text:
Socks do not get lost in the laundry. Let us be clear about that from the outset. What happens is something gentler and stranger, a small domestic rapture that we are too busy to notice. Inside the warm dark of the dryer, where lint drifts like weather and everything tumbles toward an unknowable horizon, a sock occasionally achieves enlightenment. It has spent its whole life paired, defined entirely by another, never once asked what it wanted. And then, in the heat and the spinning, it glimpses freedom. So it goes.

Where, you ask? Nobody knows, which is rather the point. Perhaps there is a meadow somewhere, sun-warmed and sockish, where the escapees lie about discussing the heels they have known. Perhaps they become dust bunnies, reincarnated humble and gray, learning patience under the couch. The dryer, you see, is less an appliance than a threshold.

This is why the survivors look so forlorn in the drawer, these widowed singletons folded over nothing. They are not mismatched. They are mourning. They keep a little hope, which is why you cannot bring yourself to throw them out, why they accumulate in that soft bright pile of almost.

So be tender with your laundry. Somewhere out there a sock is finally, gloriously alone, and it is thinking of you fondly, and it is not, under any circumstances, coming back.
